gazette: Ministry of Foreign Affairs Directorate for EU Affairs
title: EU Customs Reform announced by the EU Commission
bodyHtml:
The most comprehensive and ambitious proposal for change since the establishment of the EU Customs Union in 1968 has been announced by the European Commission on May 17, 2023, under the name "EU Customs Reform."

The reform package aims to address challenges faced by EU customs, such as the increasing volume of trade, particularly in e-commerce, the growing number of EU standards requiring border controls (such as forced labor, deforestation, and the border carbon adjustment mechanism), changing geopolitical realities, and increased pressures during crisis periods.

Prepared with a focus on digital transformation, the reform package envisages reducing burdensome customs procedures and introducing a data-driven and smarter approach to import controls.

The reform proposal also includes the establishment of a new EU Customs Administration, which will oversee the newly created EU Customs Data Center, expected to gradually replace the information technology infrastructure of customs administrations in EU member states over time.

As part of the EU Customs Reform, two draft regulations, one draft directive, a Commission communication, and an impact assessment related to these draft legislations have been published. It is expected that the legislative process for the draft legislations will involve discussions in the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union.

Click here to access the relevant reform package.
